title: Shoulder Dislocation
oneLiner: Traumatic loss of glenohumeral articulation – most common large-joint dislocation – risk of neurovascular injury and recurrence, especially in young active patients.

sections.1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You:
**Classic vignette:** A 22-year-old rugby player presents to ED after a tackle, holding his right arm with the opposite hand. The arm is slightly abducted and externally rotated. He has severe pain and cannot move the shoulder. You note a loss of the normal deltoid contour – the shoulder looks “square” rather than rounded – and you can palpate a fullness anteriorly. He complains of “pins and needles” over the lateral aspect of the shoulder.

**Dissecting the vignette:**
- **Young male, contact sport** → high-energy trauma, typical anterior dislocation mechanism (abduction + external rotation)
- **Arm held in slight abduction and external rotation** → humeral head has displaced anteriorly and inferiorly; patient can’t actively internally rotate because the head rides out of the glenoid
- **Loss of deltoid contour / “square shoulder”** → the deltoid muscle is draped over an empty glenoid; normal rounded bulge of the humeral head is absent
- **Palpable anterior fullness** → humeral head lies anteriorly (subcoracoid or subclavicular position)
- **Pins and needles over lateral shoulder** → classic “regimental badge” distribution = axillary nerve dysfunction (common injury, usually neuropraxia)
- **Inability to move shoulder** – pain and mechanical block

**Atypical presentations:**
1. **Elderly patient after a fall on outstretched hand** – may have a **posterior dislocation** (rare, 2-4%). Arm is internally rotated and adducted, no anterior bulge. Often missed; check for a “lightbulb sign” on AP radiograph.
2. **Seizure or electrical shock** – posterior dislocation because of strong internal rotator muscle contraction (latissimus dorsi, subscapularis).
3. **Recurrent dislocator with minimal trauma** – e.g. a teenager who dislocates while rolling over in bed. This hints at capsulolabral pathology (Bankart lesion) rather than high-energy avulsion.

---
sections.2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Ma:
**Plain-language definition:** A shoulder dislocation is the complete separation of the humeral head from the glenoid fossa of the scapula. It is almost always traumatic (95% anterior) and can damage nerves, arteries, and the bony/labral structures that stabilise the joint.

**Why it matters:**
- **Frequency:** Shoulder dislocations account for ~50% of all major joint dislocations.
- **Stakes:** Missed axillary nerve injury leads to permanent deltoid weakness and shoulder drop. Missed axillary artery injury (rare but limb-threatening) can cause ischaemia. Recurrence risk in young patients is high (80-90% under 20 years) – proper initial management reduces long-term disability and need for surgery.
- **Common pitfalls:** Attempting relocation without adequate analgesia/muscle relaxation; failing to document neurovascular status before and after reduction; discharging without appropriate immobilisation or follow-up.

---
sections.3. Pathophysiology — The “Why” Behind Th:
**The story:**
The glenohumeral joint is inherently unstable – the glenoid is shallow, like a golf tee. Stability relies on the labrum (fibrocartilaginous rim), capsuloligamentous complex (especially inferior glenohumeral ligament), and rotator cuff muscles. A traumatic force – typically forced **abduction + external rotation** – levers the humeral head out anteriorly, tearing the labrum (Bankart lesion) and/or stretching the capsule.

**Mechanism → signs:**
- **Abduction + external rotation lever** → humeral head exits anteriorly and inferiorly, coming to rest under the coracoid (subcoracoid) or rarely under the clavicle (subclavicular). The **coracoid tip** becomes prominent, and the humeral head is palpable anteriorly.
- **Loss of deltoid contour** → the deltoid muscle belly has lost its underlying bony support (empty glenoid). It falls flat, creating the “square” appearance.
- **Arm held in slight abduction** (10-20°) and **external rotation** → the greater tuberosity of the humerus is now posterior to the glenoid; any attempt at internal rotation is blocked by the humeral head contacting the anterior glenoid rim.
- **Axillary nerve injury** – the nerve wraps around the surgical neck of the humerus and is stretched as the humeral head displaces anteriorly. The nerve supplies deltoid (elevation) and teres minor (external rotation) as well as the “regimental badge” skin patch. Compression/neuropraxia causes sensory loss and weakness; rarely it is an axonotmesis.
- **Vascular injury** (axillary artery) – the artery is tethered at the lateral border of the first rib; anterior displacement of the head can kink or tear it, especially in elderly patients with atherosclerotic vessels. Signs: absent radial pulse, pallor, cold limb, expanding axillary haematoma.

**Connection to pattern recognition:**
The **young athlete’s dislocation** usually produces a Bankart lesion (labral tear) – recurrence risk high. The **elderly osteoporotic patient** more often fractures the humeral neck or greater tuberosity before the labrum tears (hence less recurrence but more rotator cuff tears). The **seizure patient** has violent internal rotator co-contraction causing posterior dislocation (internal rotation + adduction).

---
sections.4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins):
| Category | Cause | Stem clue |
|----------|-------|-----------|
| **Traumatic (most common)** | Anterior: fall on outstretched hand, tackle, collision | “Arm in abduction and external rotation, feels like shoulder ‘popped out’” |
| **Traumatic (uncommon)** | Posterior: fall on flexed elbow, electric shock, seizure | “Arm held in internal rotation, inability to externally rotate” |
| **Recurrent/Atraumatic** | Capsular laxity, multidirectional instability (MDI) (often bilateral) | “Bilateral shoulder pain with overhead activity – no frank dislocation but sensation of ‘slipping’” |
| **Congenital/Developmental** | Glenoid dysplasia, Ehlers-Danlos syndrome | “Multiple joint dislocations, hypermobile skin, easy bruising” |
| **Iatrogenic** | Aggressive passive range of motion under anaesthesia | “Shoulder dislocated during arthroscopy or post-operative manipulation” |

**Most common cause:** **Anterior dislocation from trauma** in young active males (85-90% of all shoulder dislocations).

---
sections.5. Investigations — What, Why, What It M:
**Diagnostic criteria:** Clinical diagnosis based on history and exam. Radiographs confirm the direction and rule out fractures.

**Imaging:**

| Test | What it shows | Why order | Interpretation |
|------|---------------|-----------|----------------|
| **AP shoulder (true AP / Grashey view)** | Humeral head overlapping or above glenoid – may show "lightbulb sign" (posterior dislocation) or obvious anterior displacement | Confirm dislocation and identify associated fractures (greater tuberosity, glenoid rim) | Humeral head lies *inferior* to coracoid (anterior) or *centered but locked* with internal rotation (posterior) |
| **Axillary lateral view** (or Velpeau axillary) | Shows position of humeral head relative to glenoid – best for posterior dislocations | Essential to confirm direction; often omitted in ED → missed posterior dislocations | Head is anterior (anterior dislocation) or posterior (posterior dislocation) |
| **West Point view** (glenoid profile) | Glenoid rim fractures (Bankart lesion) or loose bodies | Only if planning surgery; not routine | Fracture of anterior-inferior glenoid rim = bony Bankart |
| **CT shoulder** (if complex) | Fracture patterns, glenoid version, humeral head impression (Hill-Sachs defect) | Failed closed reduction, suspicion of fracture-dislocation, recurrent instability | Hill-Sachs: posterolateral humeral head compression defect from impaction on glenoid rim |
| **MRI shoulder** (elective) | Labral tears (Bankart, SLAP), rotator cuff integrity | After first reduction if recurrence risk is high; pre-operative planning | Full-thickness labral tear = Bankart lesion |

**Pattern-recognition: which single finding confirms?**
- **Anterior dislocation:** On AP radiograph, the humeral head lies inferior and medial to the glenoid – looks like a "drooping" head.
- **Posterior dislocation:** The humeral head is symmetrically rounded (“lightbulb sign”) and the normal elliptical overlap with glenoid is lost.

**What rules out?** A normal axillary lateral view with the humeral head well-centered in the glenoid effectively excludes dislocation.

**Labs:** Not needed unless suspecting sepsis (fever, elevated inflammatory markers) – septic arthritis can mimic dislocation but is rare.

---
sections.Immediate (first hour):
- **Analgesia & muscle relaxation** – key to successful reduction without further injury. Options:
  - **IV analgesia:** Morphine 5-10 mg IV (titrate) OR fentanyl 50-100 mcg IV
  - **Sedation:** Midazolam 2-5 mg IV (titrate to effect) with flumazenil available
  - **Intra-articular lidocaine:** 1% lidocaine 10-20 mL injected into joint (alternative to sedation) – provides anaesthesia but not muscle relaxation
- **Closed reduction** – multiple techniques; choose based on comfort and patient’s muscle bulk.
  - **Stimson’s technique** (prone, arm hanging off bed with 5-10 lbs traction) – slow, good for anxious patients.
  - **Milch technique** (arm abducted, externally rotated gently) – minimises force.
  - **Kocher’s technique** (flex elbow to 90°, externally rotate until reduction) – forces can fracture humerus; **avoid** with poor bone quality.
- **Post-reduction check:**
  - **Neurological:** test deltoid contraction (axillary C5-6), sensation over lateral shoulder. Document.
  - **Vascular:** palpate radial pulse, compare to opposite side, assess capillary refill.
  - **Radiograph:** confirm reduction (glenohumeral alignment) and exclude iatrogenic fracture.
sections.Ward (24-72 hours):
- **Immobilisation:** Sling and swathe (shoulder immobiliser) for 2-4 weeks in **internal rotation** (arm across chest). This allows the Bankart lesion and capsule to heal.
  - *Pitfall:* Do not use a simple sling by itself – the arm must be rotated internally to keep the humeral head pressed against the glenoid.
- **Pain control:** Paracetamol 1g PO QDS + ibuprofen 400mg PO TDS (if no contraindication) for 7-10 days.
- **Physiotherapy referral:** After immobilisation, start progressive passive range of motion (pendulum exercises at 2-4 weeks) then active-assisted → strengthening (rotator cuff and periscapular muscles). For **athletes <30 years**, early physiotherapy reduces recurrence rates.
- **Advise on future prevention:** Avoid contact sports for 6-12 weeks; consider bracing for high-risk activities (rugby, skiing).
sections.Long-term:
- **Recurrence high in young patients:** If recurrent dislocations (>1), consider **arthroscopic Bankart repair** (labral reattachment) ± capsulorrhaphy.
- **Elderly patients:** Primarily manage rotator cuff rehabilitation; surgery only for persistent pain/instability.
- **Rare but serious:** Axillary nerve injury that does not recover by 6 weeks → electromyography (EMG) ± exploration.
sections.What NOT to do:
- **Do not** attempt reduction without confirming direction – if posterior, anteriorly directed forces worsen the dislocation.
- **Do not** forget neurovascular documentation before AND after reduction – medicolegally essential.
- **Do not** immobilise an elderly patient for more than 2 weeks – risk of adhesive capsulitis (frozen shoulder).
- **Do not** discharge without ensuring follow-up (orthopaedic clinic in 2-3 weeks) and clear red-flag advice.

---
sections.7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison T:
| Feature | Shoulder Dislocation (Anterior) | AC Joint Separation | Proximal Humerus Fracture | Glenohumeral Subluxation (Transient) |
|---------|--------------------------------|---------------------|---------------------------|--------------------------------------|
| **Pain** | Sharp, severe on movement, worse with internal rotation | Localised to AC joint, superior clavicle pain | Diffuse upper arm pain, worse with any shoulder motion | Vague ache, “catching” sensation with overhead activity |
| **Exam** | Loss of deltoid contour, humeral head anteriorly palpable, arm held in ER | “Step-off” deformity at AC joint, tender on palpation; cross-arm adduction test positive | No visible deformity unless displaced; crepitus; ecchymosis develops later | No deformity; positive apprehension test (abduction+ER reproduces feeling of instability) |
| **Radiograph (AP)** | Humeral head inferior to glenoid; no fracture unless associated | Coracoclavicular distance >12mm (type III+) or clavicle elevated; normal glenohumeral joint | Fracture line through surgical/ anatomical neck; greater tuberosity possibly displaced | Normal alignment; may see small Hill-Sachs defect if chronic |
| **Labs** | Normal | Normal | Normal | Normal |
| **Key differentiating clue** | Palpable anterior humeral head; “square shoulder” | “Piano key” movement of clavicle; no loss of glenohumeral contour | Inability to actively abduct arm even after pain control; localised tenderness to proximal humerus | History of recurrent “slipping” or “giving way” without frank dislocation |

**Other mimics:** Scapular fracture (rare; scapular body tenderness, crepitus), Supraspinatus tendon rupture (weakness but no deformity), Septic arthritis (fever, inability to move even passively, raised WCC/CRP).

---
sections.8. Complications — What If You Miss It?:
| Complication | Presentation | Key signs | What to do |
|--------------|--------------|-----------|------------|
| **Axillary nerve injury** | Numbness over lateral shoulder, deltoid weakness (cannot abduct >15°) | Check deltoid bulk and “regimental badge” sensation pre & post reduction | If no recovery at 6 weeks → EMG/nerve conduction. Surgical exploration if no signs by 3 months. |
| **Axillary artery injury** | Pallor, pulselessness, pain, paraesthesia, paralysis, expanding haematoma | Absent radial pulse, cold arm, decreased BP in affected arm | **Emergency vascular consult.** Do not reduce until vascular control available. Angiography ± repair. |
| **Recurrent dislocation** | Repeated dislocations with less force – common <30 years | Apprehension (+) with abduction/ER | Arthroscopic Bankart repair ± remplissage for Hill-Sachs. |
| **Hill-Sachs defect** (impaction fracture) | May cause “engaging” instability – pain and click during external rotation | CT/MRI shows posterior humeral head defect >20% of articular surface | If engaging → arthroscopic remplissage (infraspinatus tenodesis into defect) |
| **Bankart lesion** (labral tear) | Persistent pain, feeling of instability, clicking after reduction | MRI arthrogram shows anteroinferior labral tear | Arthroscopic repair if recurrent dislocations. |
| **Rotator cuff tear** (especially in elderly) | Weakness of abduction/external rotation that persists >2 weeks | Positive drop-arm test, weakness on resisted external rotation | MRI ultrasound; surgical repair if acute tear in active patient. |
| **Frozen shoulder (adhesive capsulitis)** | Gradual loss of passive range of motion, especially external rotation | Global stiffness, “collar of pain” | Physiotherapy, corticosteroid injection, hydrodilatation. Avoid prolonged immobilisation. |

**Late-presentation patterns:** A patient who presents days after failed reduction attempt with worsening axillary pain and new hand paraesthesia may have a compressive neuropathy from an enlarged haematoma or false aneurysm.

---
sections.9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls:
**Mnemonics:**

- **“4 P’s” of axillary nerve injury:** Pain, Pins and needles (over deltoid patch), Paralysis (deltoid weakness), Pulselessness? (no – that’s axillary artery) → Actually: **Sensory loss = Patch, Motor = Deltoid, Teres minor** → remembered as **“SAD”** (Sensory Axillary Deltoid)
- **“RICE” for reduction sequence:** Relaxation (analgesia/sedation), Identify direction (X-ray), Choose technique (Stimson/Milch), Check post-reduction (neurovascular + X-ray)
- **“Bankart = Bottom front”** – the labral tear is at the anterior-inferior quadrant of the glenoid (the bottom front part).

**Clinical pearls:**
1. **Always X-ray before and after reduction** – medicolegally mandatory and prevents missing associated fractures.
2. **Document neurovascular status before ANY manipulation** – if nerve injury is present pre-reduction, you’ve saved yourself from blame.
3. **Younger patients (<30) have high recurrence** – counsel about surgical indications early. Older patients (>60) have low recurrence but high risk of rotator cuff tear.
4. **Posterior dislocation looks like a “lightbulb”** on AP – the humeral head appears symmetrically round because it’s pointing straight at the X-ray beam.
5. **Intra-articular lidocaine is as effective as procedural sedation** for reduction in many studies – useful when resources limited or patient prefers less sedation.
6. **The “apprehension sign”** (patient resists abduction/ER) is not for acute dislocation but for chronic instability – test it only when pain has subsided (after reduction).
7. **Recurrent dislocators may reduce themselves** – do not force their arm; they often have a specific technique.

**Common mistakes:**
- Attempting reduction in awake, un-sedated patient – leads to muscle guarding, iatrogenic fracture.
- Using heavy traction (e.g. traction-countertraction) in osteoporotic bone – **fracture risk high**.
- Discharging with simple triangular sling (arm in neutral rotation) – should be **internal rotation sling**.
- Missing posterior dislocation because AP X-ray looks “normal” – always get axillary lateral.

---
sections.10. Escalation Criteria:
| Situation | Action | Threshold |
|-----------|--------|-----------|
| **Axillary artery injury** (absent radial pulse, cold limb, expanding haematoma) | **Immediate senior orthopaedic & vascular consult.** Do not reduce until vascular team present. | Any clinical suspicion → Code Blue / trauma call |
| **Failed closed reduction** (3 attempts or >30 minutes) | **Call orthopaedic registrar.** May need reduction under general anaesthetic or CT to exclude interposed soft tissue (e.g. biceps tendon, labrum). | >2 attempts in ED = senior help |
| **Suspected fracture-dislocation** (e.g. surgical neck fracture, glenoid rim fracture >25%) | **Call orthopaedic SpR.** May require operative reduction and fixation. | Visible fracture on X-ray + dislocation |
| **Neurological deficit that does not resolve post-reduction** | **Urgent neurology referral / nerve conduction study.** If complete axillary nerve palsy with no recovery by 6 weeks → surgical exploration. | New or worsening numbness/weakness |
| **Signs of sepsis** (fever, rigors, high WCC/CRP) with shoulder pain and reduced range | **Septic arthritis is a surgical emergency.** Call orthopaedics for urgent aspiration/debridement. | Temperature >38°C, lactate >2, NEWS2 ≥5 |
| **Worsening pain after reduction** (suspect compartment syndrome or iatrogenic fracture) | **Repeat X-ray, check neurovascular obs.** If severe pain with passive stretch, tense swelling → fasciotomy consult. | Compartment pressure >30 mmHg OR loss of pulse |

**General escalation:** If NEWS2 ≥7 or lactate >4 mmol/L, activate Sepsis 6 and critical care review – even without obvious dislocation pathology.

---

**Final take-home message:** Shoulder dislocation is common and usually straightforward, but the devil is in the details – always image, always reduce with proper analgesia, always check neurovascular status, and always immobilise in internal rotation. The young patient needs a different plan than the elderly one. And never ever forget the axillary nerve.

rawMarkdown:
# Shoulder Dislocation

**One-liner:** Traumatic loss of glenohumeral articulation – most common large-joint dislocation – risk of neurovascular injury and recurrence, especially in young active patients.

---

## 1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You'll See

**Classic vignette:** A 22-year-old rugby player presents to ED after a tackle, holding his right arm with the opposite hand. The arm is slightly abducted and externally rotated. He has severe pain and cannot move the shoulder. You note a loss of the normal deltoid contour – the shoulder looks “square” rather than rounded – and you can palpate a fullness anteriorly. He complains of “pins and needles” over the lateral aspect of the shoulder.

**Dissecting the vignette:**
- **Young male, contact sport** → high-energy trauma, typical anterior dislocation mechanism (abduction + external rotation)
- **Arm held in slight abduction and external rotation** → humeral head has displaced anteriorly and inferiorly; patient can’t actively internally rotate because the head rides out of the glenoid
- **Loss of deltoid contour / “square shoulder”** → the deltoid muscle is draped over an empty glenoid; normal rounded bulge of the humeral head is absent
- **Palpable anterior fullness** → humeral head lies anteriorly (subcoracoid or subclavicular position)
- **Pins and needles over lateral shoulder** → classic “regimental badge” distribution = axillary nerve dysfunction (common injury, usually neuropraxia)
- **Inability to move shoulder** – pain and mechanical block

**Atypical presentations:**
1. **Elderly patient after a fall on outstretched hand** – may have a **posterior dislocation** (rare, 2-4%). Arm is internally rotated and adducted, no anterior bulge. Often missed; check for a “lightbulb sign” on AP radiograph.
2. **Seizure or electrical shock** – posterior dislocation because of strong internal rotator muscle contraction (latissimus dorsi, subscapularis).
3. **Recurrent dislocator with minimal trauma** – e.g. a teenager who dislocates while rolling over in bed. This hints at capsulolabral pathology (Bankart lesion) rather than high-energy avulsion.

---

## 2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Matters)

**Plain-language definition:** A shoulder dislocation is the complete separation of the humeral head from the glenoid fossa of the scapula. It is almost always traumatic (95% anterior) and can damage nerves, arteries, and the bony/labral structures that stabilise the joint.

**Why it matters:**
- **Frequency:** Shoulder dislocations account for ~50% of all major joint dislocations.
- **Stakes:** Missed axillary nerve injury leads to permanent deltoid weakness and shoulder drop. Missed axillary artery injury (rare but limb-threatening) can cause ischaemia. Recurrence risk in young patients is high (80-90% under 20 years) – proper initial management reduces long-term disability and need for surgery.
- **Common pitfalls:** Attempting relocation without adequate analgesia/muscle relaxation; failing to document neurovascular status before and after reduction; discharging without appropriate immobilisation or follow-up.

---

## 3. Pathophysiology — The “Why” Behind The Findings

**The story:**
The glenohumeral joint is inherently unstable – the glenoid is shallow, like a golf tee. Stability relies on the labrum (fibrocartilaginous rim), capsuloligamentous complex (especially inferior glenohumeral ligament), and rotator cuff muscles. A traumatic force – typically forced **abduction + external rotation** – levers the humeral head out anteriorly, tearing the labrum (Bankart lesion) and/or stretching the capsule.

**Mechanism → signs:**
- **Abduction + external rotation lever** → humeral head exits anteriorly and inferiorly, coming to rest under the coracoid (subcoracoid) or rarely under the clavicle (subclavicular). The **coracoid tip** becomes prominent, and the humeral head is palpable anteriorly.
- **Loss of deltoid contour** → the deltoid muscle belly has lost its underlying bony support (empty glenoid). It falls flat, creating the “square” appearance.
- **Arm held in slight abduction** (10-20°) and **external rotation** → the greater tuberosity of the humerus is now posterior to the glenoid; any attempt at internal rotation is blocked by the humeral head contacting the anterior glenoid rim.
- **Axillary nerve injury** – the nerve wraps around the surgical neck of the humerus and is stretched as the humeral head displaces anteriorly. The nerve supplies deltoid (elevation) and teres minor (external rotation) as well as the “regimental badge” skin patch. Compression/neuropraxia causes sensory loss and weakness; rarely it is an axonotmesis.
- **Vascular injury** (axillary artery) – the artery is tethered at the lateral border of the first rib; anterior displacement of the head can kink or tear it, especially in elderly patients with atherosclerotic vessels. Signs: absent radial pulse, pallor, cold limb, expanding axillary haematoma.

**Connection to pattern recognition:**
The **young athlete’s dislocation** usually produces a Bankart lesion (labral tear) – recurrence risk high. The **elderly osteoporotic patient** more often fractures the humeral neck or greater tuberosity before the labrum tears (hence less recurrence but more rotator cuff tears). The **seizure patient** has violent internal rotator co-contraction causing posterior dislocation (internal rotation + adduction).

---

## 4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins)

| Category | Cause | Stem clue |
|----------|-------|-----------|
| **Traumatic (most common)** | Anterior: fall on outstretched hand, tackle, collision | “Arm in abduction and external rotation, feels like shoulder ‘popped out’” |
| **Traumatic (uncommon)** | Posterior: fall on flexed elbow, electric shock, seizure | “Arm held in internal rotation, inability to externally rotate” |
| **Recurrent/Atraumatic** | Capsular laxity, multidirectional instability (MDI) (often bilateral) | “Bilateral shoulder pain with overhead activity – no frank dislocation but sensation of ‘slipping’” |
| **Congenital/Developmental** | Glenoid dysplasia, Ehlers-Danlos syndrome | “Multiple joint dislocations, hypermobile skin, easy bruising” |
| **Iatrogenic** | Aggressive passive range of motion under anaesthesia | “Shoulder dislocated during arthroscopy or post-operative manipulation” |

**Most common cause:** **Anterior dislocation from trauma** in young active males (85-90% of all shoulder dislocations).

---

## 5. Investigations — What, Why, What It Means

**Diagnostic criteria:** Clinical diagnosis based on history and exam. Radiographs confirm the direction and rule out fractures.

**Imaging:**

| Test | What it shows | Why order | Interpretation |
|------|---------------|-----------|----------------|
| **AP shoulder (true AP / Grashey view)** | Humeral head overlapping or above glenoid – may show "lightbulb sign" (posterior dislocation) or obvious anterior displacement | Confirm dislocation and identify associated fractures (greater tuberosity, glenoid rim) | Humeral head lies *inferior* to coracoid (anterior) or *centered but locked* with internal rotation (posterior) |
| **Axillary lateral view** (or Velpeau axillary) | Shows position of humeral head relative to glenoid – best for posterior dislocations | Essential to confirm direction; often omitted in ED → missed posterior dislocations | Head is anterior (anterior dislocation) or posterior (posterior dislocation) |
| **West Point view** (glenoid profile) | Glenoid rim fractures (Bankart lesion) or loose bodies | Only if planning surgery; not routine | Fracture of anterior-inferior glenoid rim = bony Bankart |
| **CT shoulder** (if complex) | Fracture patterns, glenoid version, humeral head impression (Hill-Sachs defect) | Failed closed reduction, suspicion of fracture-dislocation, recurrent instability | Hill-Sachs: posterolateral humeral head compression defect from impaction on glenoid rim |
| **MRI shoulder** (elective) | Labral tears (Bankart, SLAP), rotator cuff integrity | After first reduction if recurrence risk is high; pre-operative planning | Full-thickness labral tear = Bankart lesion |

**Pattern-recognition: which single finding confirms?**
- **Anterior dislocation:** On AP radiograph, the humeral head lies inferior and medial to the glenoid – looks like a "drooping" head.
- **Posterior dislocation:** The humeral head is symmetrically rounded (“lightbulb sign”) and the normal elliptical overlap with glenoid is lost.

**What rules out?** A normal axillary lateral view with the humeral head well-centered in the glenoid effectively excludes dislocation.

**Labs:** Not needed unless suspecting sepsis (fever, elevated inflammatory markers) – septic arthritis can mimic dislocation but is rare.

---

## 6. Management — Approach & Reasoning

### Immediate (first hour)

- **Analgesia & muscle relaxation** – key to successful reduction without further injury. Options:
  - **IV analgesia:** Morphine 5-10 mg IV (titrate) OR fentanyl 50-100 mcg IV
  - **Sedation:** Midazolam 2-5 mg IV (titrate to effect) with flumazenil available
  - **Intra-articular lidocaine:** 1% lidocaine 10-20 mL injected into joint (alternative to sedation) – provides anaesthesia but not muscle relaxation
- **Closed reduction** – multiple techniques; choose based on comfort and patient’s muscle bulk.
  - **Stimson’s technique** (prone, arm hanging off bed with 5-10 lbs traction) – slow, good for anxious patients.
  - **Milch technique** (arm abducted, externally rotated gently) – minimises force.
  - **Kocher’s technique** (flex elbow to 90°, externally rotate until reduction) – forces can fracture humerus; **avoid** with poor bone quality.
- **Post-reduction check:**
  - **Neurological:** test deltoid contraction (axillary C5-6), sensation over lateral shoulder. Document.
  - **Vascular:** palpate radial pulse, compare to opposite side, assess capillary refill.
  - **Radiograph:** confirm reduction (glenohumeral alignment) and exclude iatrogenic fracture.

### Ward (24-72 hours)

- **Immobilisation:** Sling and swathe (shoulder immobiliser) for 2-4 weeks in **internal rotation** (arm across chest). This allows the Bankart lesion and capsule to heal.
  - *Pitfall:* Do not use a simple sling by itself – the arm must be rotated internally to keep the humeral head pressed against the glenoid.
- **Pain control:** Paracetamol 1g PO QDS + ibuprofen 400mg PO TDS (if no contraindication) for 7-10 days.
- **Physiotherapy referral:** After immobilisation, start progressive passive range of motion (pendulum exercises at 2-4 weeks) then active-assisted → strengthening (rotator cuff and periscapular muscles). For **athletes <30 years**, early physiotherapy reduces recurrence rates.
- **Advise on future prevention:** Avoid contact sports for 6-12 weeks; consider bracing for high-risk activities (rugby, skiing).

### Long-term

- **Recurrence high in young patients:** If recurrent dislocations (>1), consider **arthroscopic Bankart repair** (labral reattachment) ± capsulorrhaphy.
- **Elderly patients:** Primarily manage rotator cuff rehabilitation; surgery only for persistent pain/instability.
- **Rare but serious:** Axillary nerve injury that does not recover by 6 weeks → electromyography (EMG) ± exploration.

### What NOT to do
- **Do not** attempt reduction without confirming direction – if posterior, anteriorly directed forces worsen the dislocation.
- **Do not** forget neurovascular documentation before AND after reduction – medicolegally essential.
- **Do not** immobilise an elderly patient for more than 2 weeks – risk of adhesive capsulitis (frozen shoulder).
- **Do not** discharge without ensuring follow-up (orthopaedic clinic in 2-3 weeks) and clear red-flag advice.

---

## 7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison Table

| Feature | Shoulder Dislocation (Anterior) | AC Joint Separation | Proximal Humerus Fracture | Glenohumeral Subluxation (Transient) |
|---------|--------------------------------|---------------------|---------------------------|--------------------------------------|
| **Pain** | Sharp, severe on movement, worse with internal rotation | Localised to AC joint, superior clavicle pain | Diffuse upper arm pain, worse with any shoulder motion | Vague ache, “catching” sensation with overhead activity |
| **Exam** | Loss of deltoid contour, humeral head anteriorly palpable, arm held in ER | “Step-off” deformity at AC joint, tender on palpation; cross-arm adduction test positive | No visible deformity unless displaced; crepitus; ecchymosis develops later | No deformity; positive apprehension test (abduction+ER reproduces feeling of instability) |
| **Radiograph (AP)** | Humeral head inferior to glenoid; no fracture unless associated | Coracoclavicular distance >12mm (type III+) or clavicle elevated; normal glenohumeral joint | Fracture line through surgical/ anatomical neck; greater tuberosity possibly displaced | Normal alignment; may see small Hill-Sachs defect if chronic |
| **Labs** | Normal | Normal | Normal | Normal |
| **Key differentiating clue** | Palpable anterior humeral head; “square shoulder” | “Piano key” movement of clavicle; no loss of glenohumeral contour | Inability to actively abduct arm even after pain control; localised tenderness to proximal humerus | History of recurrent “slipping” or “giving way” without frank dislocation |

**Other mimics:** Scapular fracture (rare; scapular body tenderness, crepitus), Supraspinatus tendon rupture (weakness but no deformity), Septic arthritis (fever, inability to move even passively, raised WCC/CRP).

---

## 8. Complications — What If You Miss It?

| Complication | Presentation | Key signs | What to do |
|--------------|--------------|-----------|------------|
| **Axillary nerve injury** | Numbness over lateral shoulder, deltoid weakness (cannot abduct >15°) | Check deltoid bulk and “regimental badge” sensation pre & post reduction | If no recovery at 6 weeks → EMG/nerve conduction. Surgical exploration if no signs by 3 months. |
| **Axillary artery injury** | Pallor, pulselessness, pain, paraesthesia, paralysis, expanding haematoma | Absent radial pulse, cold arm, decreased BP in affected arm | **Emergency vascular consult.** Do not reduce until vascular control available. Angiography ± repair. |
| **Recurrent dislocation** | Repeated dislocations with less force – common <30 years | Apprehension (+) with abduction/ER | Arthroscopic Bankart repair ± remplissage for Hill-Sachs. |
| **Hill-Sachs defect** (impaction fracture) | May cause “engaging” instability – pain and click during external rotation | CT/MRI shows posterior humeral head defect >20% of articular surface | If engaging → arthroscopic remplissage (infraspinatus tenodesis into defect) |
| **Bankart lesion** (labral tear) | Persistent pain, feeling of instability, clicking after reduction | MRI arthrogram shows anteroinferior labral tear | Arthroscopic repair if recurrent dislocations. |
| **Rotator cuff tear** (especially in elderly) | Weakness of abduction/external rotation that persists >2 weeks | Positive drop-arm test, weakness on resisted external rotation | MRI ultrasound; surgical repair if acute tear in active patient. |
| **Frozen shoulder (adhesive capsulitis)** | Gradual loss of passive range of motion, especially external rotation | Global stiffness, “collar of pain” | Physiotherapy, corticosteroid injection, hydrodilatation. Avoid prolonged immobilisation. |

**Late-presentation patterns:** A patient who presents days after failed reduction attempt with worsening axillary pain and new hand paraesthesia may have a compressive neuropathy from an enlarged haematoma or false aneurysm.

---

## 9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls

**Mnemonics:**

- **“4 P’s” of axillary nerve injury:** Pain, Pins and needles (over deltoid patch), Paralysis (deltoid weakness), Pulselessness? (no – that’s axillary artery) → Actually: **Sensory loss = Patch, Motor = Deltoid, Teres minor** → remembered as **“SAD”** (Sensory Axillary Deltoid)
- **“RICE” for reduction sequence:** Relaxation (analgesia/sedation), Identify direction (X-ray), Choose technique (Stimson/Milch), Check post-reduction (neurovascular + X-ray)
- **“Bankart = Bottom front”** – the labral tear is at the anterior-inferior quadrant of the glenoid (the bottom front part).

**Clinical pearls:**
1. **Always X-ray before and after reduction** – medicolegally mandatory and prevents missing associated fractures.
2. **Document neurovascular status before ANY manipulation** – if nerve injury is present pre-reduction, you’ve saved yourself from blame.
3. **Younger patients (<30) have high recurrence** – counsel about surgical indications early. Older patients (>60) have low recurrence but high risk of rotator cuff tear.
4. **Posterior dislocation looks like a “lightbulb”** on AP – the humeral head appears symmetrically round because it’s pointing straight at the X-ray beam.
5. **Intra-articular lidocaine is as effective as procedural sedation** for reduction in many studies – useful when resources limited or patient prefers less sedation.
6. **The “apprehension sign”** (patient resists abduction/ER) is not for acute dislocation but for chronic instability – test it only when pain has subsided (after reduction).
7. **Recurrent dislocators may reduce themselves** – do not force their arm; they often have a specific technique.

**Common mistakes:**
- Attempting reduction in awake, un-sedated patient – leads to muscle guarding, iatrogenic fracture.
- Using heavy traction (e.g. traction-countertraction) in osteoporotic bone – **fracture risk high**.
- Discharging with simple triangular sling (arm in neutral rotation) – should be **internal rotation sling**.
- Missing posterior dislocation because AP X-ray looks “normal” – always get axillary lateral.

---

## 10. Escalation Criteria

| Situation | Action | Threshold |
|-----------|--------|-----------|
| **Axillary artery injury** (absent radial pulse, cold limb, expanding haematoma) | **Immediate senior orthopaedic & vascular consult.** Do not reduce until vascular team present. | Any clinical suspicion → Code Blue / trauma call |
| **Failed closed reduction** (3 attempts or >30 minutes) | **Call orthopaedic registrar.** May need reduction under general anaesthetic or CT to exclude interposed soft tissue (e.g. biceps tendon, labrum). | >2 attempts in ED = senior help |
| **Suspected fracture-dislocation** (e.g. surgical neck fracture, glenoid rim fracture >25%) | **Call orthopaedic SpR.** May require operative reduction and fixation. | Visible fracture on X-ray + dislocation |
| **Neurological deficit that does not resolve post-reduction** | **Urgent neurology referral / nerve conduction study.** If complete axillary nerve palsy with no recovery by 6 weeks → surgical exploration. | New or worsening numbness/weakness |
| **Signs of sepsis** (fever, rigors, high WCC/CRP) with shoulder pain and reduced range | **Septic arthritis is a surgical emergency.** Call orthopaedics for urgent aspiration/debridement. | Temperature >38°C, lactate >2, NEWS2 ≥5 |
| **Worsening pain after reduction** (suspect compartment syndrome or iatrogenic fracture) | **Repeat X-ray, check neurovascular obs.** If severe pain with passive stretch, tense swelling → fasciotomy consult. | Compartment pressure >30 mmHg OR loss of pulse |

**General escalation:** If NEWS2 ≥7 or lactate >4 mmol/L, activate Sepsis 6 and critical care review – even without obvious dislocation pathology.

---

**Final take-home message:** Shoulder dislocation is common and usually straightforward, but the devil is in the details – always image, always reduce with proper analgesia, always check neurovascular status, and always immobilise in internal rotation. The young patient needs a different plan than the elderly one. And never ever forget the axillary nerve.
